content.full_text:
Re:
Fnrn Por-rrrcer- PRACTrces CoMMrssroN
.128 J Street o Suite 620 r Sacramento. CA 9-5914-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
June 1, 2010
Justin Paddock, Air Pollution Specialist
Planning & Technical Support Division
California Air Resources Board
1001 I Street, 7-848
Sacramento, California 958 l4
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-075
Dear Mr. Paddock:
This letter is in response to your request for. advice regarding the honorarium
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This letter is based solely on the facts
presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as the
finder of fact when it renders advice. (ln re Ogtesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Also, our
advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the
application of any incompatible activities restrictions your agency may impose. We suggest
that you confer with your agency's counsel on the issue of incompatible activities.
QUESTTON
Does the Act's prohibition on honoraria prohibit you from earning income as a law
school library research assistant if some of the work you perform is used by a professor to
publish a law review article in which you will be a co-author?
CONCLUSION
No. Because your work as a research assistant is a practice of employment in a
bona fide business, trade or profession, you are not prohibited by the Act's honoraria
prohibition from working as a research assistant and having that work used for a law
review article that you will co-author.
' The Political Retbrm Act is contained in Governmenr Code Secrions lt 1000 rhrough 91014. AII
statutory ref'erences are to the Government Code. r.rrrless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of 'Iitle 2 of the Calitirrnia Code
trf Regulations. All regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations,
unless otherwise indicated.


File No. A-10-075
Page No. 2
FACTS
You have worked for the California Air Resources Board ("ARB") since Febmary of
2007 and are currently a staff member in the Planning and Technical Support Division,
which is implementing Senate Bill 375 (Sen. nill Nol37 5 (2007-2008 R;g. sess.).)
Implementing SB 375 is considered to be one of ARB's responsibilities in order to meet the
statlrtory requirements of Assembly Bill ("AB") 32 (Assem. Bill No. 32 (2005-2006 Reg.
Sess.).)
ln addition to working for ARB you also attend McGeorge School of Law in the
evening. You work as a McGeorge Law School Law Library Research Assistant and you
sporadically work to provide citations and give professors background information for law
review articles they are working on. For this position you are not given a regular set of hours
to work, and are given assignments on an as needed basis throughout the semester.
Generally, a professor will request a research assistant from the library to help with research
and then you are assigned to the job. You sometimes work with the same proiessors on
multiple assignments and those who know that you work as a research assistant will
occasionally come directly to you for a project assignment. When a professor comes directly
to you for an assignment, you relay the message to the library and are assigned to the projeci.
For this work you are paid $14.00.per hour.
Under ARB's conflict-of-interest code the income you earn from your research
assistant position is a reportable economic interest. Recently, a professorat the law school
asked you to co-author a law review article with him regarding implementation of AB 32,
something you have developed an intimate knowledge of in your three years with ARB. you
would be paid as a library research assistant while doing research for this article (the same
position you already hold) and you plan to use only publically available information when
developing the article.
AI\[ALYSIS
Section 89502(c) provides that "no designated employee of a state or local
government agency shall accept an honorarium from any source if the member or employee
would be required to report the receipt of income or gifts from that source on his or her
statement of economic interests." As a designated employee, you are required to disclose
sources of income and gifts; therefore, you are generally prohibited under Section 89502(c)
from accepting honoraria from persons for which you have to report receipt of income and
gifts on your statement of economic interests.
Section 89501 defines the term "honorarium," in pertinent part, as follows:
"(a) For purposes of this chapter, 'honorarium' means, except as provided in
subdivision (b), any payment made in consideration for any speech given,
article published, or attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering.


File No. A-10-075
Page No. 3
"(b) The term 'honorarium' does not include:
"(1) Earned income for personal services which are customarily provided in
connection with the practice of a bona fide business, trade, or profession, such
as teaching, practicing law, medicine, insurance, real estate, banking, or
building contracting, unless the sole or predominant activity of the 6.,sir"rs,
trade, or profession is making speeches. The commission ihall adopt
regulations to implement this subdivision.,'
Interpreting Section 89501(bx l), Regulation lg93z provides:
"(a) 'Honorarium' does not include income earned from personal services if:
"(l) The services are provided in connection with an individual's business or
the individual's practice of or employment in a bona fide business, trade, or
profession, such as teaching, practicing law, medicine, insurance, real estate,
banking, or building contracting, pursuant to [Regulations 18932.1 through
18932.31; and
"(2)The services are customarily provided in cormection with the business, trade, or
profession."
Working as a research assistant in a law school library is a practice of employment in
a bona fide business, trade or profession; therefore, the honoiarium provisions do noi apply
to you with respect to your work as a research assistant. Because the only compensation you
will receive while conducting research for the law review article that you will co-author is
the hourly pay you currently receive as a research assistant with the liw school library, you
may keep your paid position as a research assistant and be a co-author for the law review
article. However, you are still required to report the income you receive from this position
on your statement of economic interests (Form 700).
If you have other questions on this matter, prease contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
/$il,,r,l^ {/, b/r'-
By: Sukhi K. Brar
Commission Counsel, Legal Division
SKB:jgl

unless otherwise indicated.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act's prohibition on honoraria prohibit you from earning income as a law
school library research assistant if some of the work you perform is used by a professor to
publish a law review article in which you will be a co-author?

CONCLUSION: No. Because your work as a research assistant is a practice of employment in a
bona fide business, trade or profession, you are not prohibited by the Act's honoraria
prohibition from working as a research assistant and having that work used for a law
review article that you will co-author.
' The Political Retbrm Act is contained in Governmenr Code Secrions lt 1000 rhrough 91014. AII
statutory ref'erences are to the Government Code. r.rrrless otherwise indicated.,
unless otherwise indicated.
